design_philosophy.core_loop: Run, jump, and puzzle-solve across side-scrolling levels using Yoshi's unique mechanics (flutter jump, egg-aiming, tongue grab, ground pound) while protecting Baby Mario from falling off
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Protecting Baby Mario from enemies and environmental hazards; timer management when Mario falls into bubble after hit
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Control a friendly dinosaur as the hero of the series, with Baby Mario as dependent companion; experience accessible yet deep platforming with new signature mechanics
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Shifts series focus from jump-precision to puzzle-solving and item-collecting; introduces hand-drawn, crayon-aesthetic art style; makes Yoshi the protagonist rather than power-up; Baby Mario dependency creates constant low-level stress and responsibility management distinct from traditional Mario games

difficulty.lives_system: Extra lives earned through coin collection; instant death from pits, spikes, lava, thorns; life loss when Baby Mario timer expires after falling off
difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based progression across 48 levels in 6 worlds; no mid-level checkpoints documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Puzzle-solving and item-collecting optional elements (hidden flowers, red coins) allow skilled players to unlock Extra stage and affect scores; collection completeness affects difficulty scaling

progression.upgrade_system: Power-ups that transform Yoshi (car, drill, helicopter, submarine) or grant abilities (watermelon shoots seeds, fire enemies create flamethrower); reserve power-up system (hold one item like +10 star or magnifying glass); six eggs can be stored simultaneously
progression.unlock_mechanism: Every fourth level features boss fight against large version of previous foe; progression unlocks new worlds; Extra stage unlocks via score/collection performance
replayability_hooks: Hidden red coins scattered throughout levels requiring exploration
Hidden flowers to find affecting stage completion
Score targets affecting Extra stage unlock
Egg variety and strategic storage (up to 6 eggs) allows different approaches to same level

world_building_techniques: Prequel narrative: establishes young Bowser as villain, explains Mario/Luigi kidnapping setup
Hand-drawn, paper-and-crayon aesthetic creates unified storybook world
Progressive environment themes (mines, ski jumps, fiery dungeons) establish distinct world sections
Boss naming convention and 'large version of previous foe' design connects enemies to world narrative

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters occur every fourth level (two per world); face enlarged variants of enemies encountered in regular levels; represent progression gates with increasing difficulty
notable_bosses: Lakitu's Wall encounter (referenced in level design)
Prince Froggy's Fort boss (referenced in level design)
Marching Milde's Fort boss (referenced in level design)
Hookbill the Koopa's Castle boss (referenced in level design)
Sluggy the Unshaven's Fort boss (referenced in level design)

faction_indicators: Yoshi's Island tribe (player allies) vs. Kamek's forces (kidnapper antagonists)
Young Bowser as emerging dark power
Stork delivery service as outside world connection
danger_signals: Kamek's initial kidnapping attack on stork establishes threat level
Baby Mario in constant peril (bubble timeout mechanic)
Environmental hazards (pits, spikes, lava, thorns) as tangible dangers
Large enemy versions as progression-gated threats
environmental_storytelling: Crayon-drawn art style establishes childlike, storybook tone
Environment variety (mines, ski slopes, castles, dungeons) shows world scope
Coloring book aesthetic reinforces narrative as young Mario brothers' origin story
Boss lair progression from local enemies to final castle shows escalating conflict
